graph_vector_fulltext.message:
The context describes an initial “floating point operations” (FLOPs) threshold as a compute-based threshold (based on the cumulative amount of computation used for training) which, if met by a general-purpose AI model, leads to a presumption that the model is a general-purpose AI model “with systemic risk.” The threshold is intended to be adjusted over time to reflect technological and industrial changes (for example, algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency) and is to be supplemented with benchmarks and indicators for model capability.

On notification: the provider should notify the AI Office at the latest two weeks after the requirements are met, or once it becomes known that the model will meet the requirements that lead to the presumption. This is described as especially relevant for the FLOPs threshold because training requires planning and upfront allocation of compute resources, so providers can typically know before training is completed whether the threshold will be met. In that notification context, the provider should be able to demonstrate that, due to specific characteristics, the model exceptionally does not present systemic risks (if they claim that exception).

Additional obligations: the context states that general-purpose AI models with systemic risk are “subject to relevant obligations,” and that providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risks must assess and mitigate systemic risks. It also indicates the AI Office monitors compliance with obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models and conducts evaluations/monitoring activities, supported by a scientific panel and a “system of qualified alerts.” The provided context does not list the full set of those additional obligations beyond assess/mitigate and the related monitoring/governance mechanisms.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: What is the floating point operations threshold and what is its notification procedure? Are there additional obligations for general-purpose AI models with systemic risk?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
The context describes an initial “floating point operations” (FLOPs) threshold as a compute-based threshold (based on the cumulative amount of computation used for training) which, if met by a general-purpose AI model, leads to a presumption that the model is a general-purpose AI model “with systemic risk.” The threshold is intended to be adjusted over time to reflect technological and industrial changes (for example, algorithmic improvements or increased hardware efficiency) and is to be supplemented with benchmarks and indicators for model capability.

On notification: the provider should notify the AI Office at the latest two weeks after the requirements are met, or once it becomes known that the model will meet the requirements that lead to the presumption. This is described as especially relevant for the FLOPs threshold because training requires planning and upfront allocation of compute resources, so providers can typically know before training is completed whether the threshold will be met. In that notification context, the provider should be able to demonstrate that, due to specific characteristics, the model exceptionally does not present systemic risks (if they claim that exception).

Additional obligations: the context states that general-purpose AI models with systemic risk are “subject to relevant obligations,” and that providers of general-purpose AI models with systemic risks must assess and mitigate systemic risks. It also indicates the AI Office monitors compliance with obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models and conducts evaluations/monitoring activities, supported by a scientific panel and a “system of qualified alerts.” The provided context does not list the full set of those additional obligations beyond assess/mitigate and the related monitoring/governance mechanisms.
